narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in higher education, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that affected your application. The admissions model evaluated your profile—including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, LSAT score of 24, first-year school decile of 1st, third-year decile of 1st, middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—against patterns from thousands of past students who took the bar exam. It predicted a 96% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which is why your application was rejected; schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking in the bottom 10%, as it stood out well below the typical student's mid-range performance and signaled potential struggles in sustaining academic momentum. Your LSAT score of 24, far lower than the average of around 37, was the next biggest driver, heavily weighing against you compared to peers who scored much higher. Your first-year decile in the bottom 10% also contributed notably to the risk assessment, reinforcing concerns about early academic fit. Lesser influences like your middle family income bracket nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, while your undergraduate GPA actually helped a bit by being closer to average, and your full-time status offered minor reassurance. Overall, your metrics placed you below typical admitted students, especially in key academic rankings and standardized testing, where stronger performers usually predict bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you've overcome—many from similar backgrounds excel with targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your grades to climb those school deciles, and seeking mentorship programs tailored for Black women in law to build a standout reapplication. You're capable of turning this around, and with focused improvements on those top areas, future models and admissions teams will see your true promise. Keep pushing—you've got this.
